observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Properly recognize the semi-expendable properties and the corresponding expense accounts by using the list of new accounts as provided under Annex B of COA Circular No. 2024-006 dated March 14, 2024; and
Retrospectively apply the newly established capitalization threshold of ₱50,000.00 by recording the necessary adjusting journal entries in the books of accounts, as follows:
General Fund Books:
Special Education Fund Books:
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GSO and the Municipal Accountant to strictly adhere with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2022-004 dated May 31, 2022, by ensuring the preparation and maintenance of the prescribed forms, registry, and reports to strengthen the controls and safeguards over semi-expendable properties.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Prepare the necessary journal entry to recognize the transfer of assets from the DPWH, as follows:
Restate the financial statements to reflect the necessary adjustments arising from the recognition of the transferred assets, to ensure the fair presentation of the Municipality’s financial position.
We also recommended that the Office of the Municipal Mayor promptly furnish all relevant documents to the Office of the Municipal Accountant and General Services Office upon receipt of PPE and their corresponding documents, to ensure the timely recognition of these assets in both the financial statements and property records.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Municipal Health Officer as the end-user, to utilize the DPRI as a reference when determining the prices of medicines in their PPMPs and PRs in order to avoid incurring excessive price quotations and ensure cost-efficient procurement of essential medicines; and
BAC and the Head of the TWG, to review the prices of medicines specified in the PRs or PPMPs. In the event where the latest published DPRI does not provide a reference price for certain essential medicines, consider using the previous year's awarded price as the ABC or conduct a market survey to determine the lowest and most competitive prices.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the PRAISE Committee to:
Submit written justification on the computation and granting of the 2024 PRAISE incentive, which included newly hired employees;
Attend orientation and capacity-building activities to avoid misinterpretation of applicable policies and ensure consistent, fair, and compliant implementation of the PRAISE system; and
Immediately submit the Annual PRAISE Report for CY 2024 to the CSC Regional Office, a copy furnished to this office, and submit future reports on or before the 30th day of January of the following year.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer to:
Adhere to the mandatory installation of project signboards of all infrastructure projects and devise a system for the periodic updating of project signboards using the schedule prescribed under Section 3 of COA Circular No. 2013-004, to accurately reflect the progress of infrastructure projects and to uphold the principles of transparency and public accountability;
Support all individual POWs with Detailed Cost Estimates, including itemized breakdowns and technical specifications for all project components to ensure accuracy, transparency and value for money; and
Evaluate and compare the CSHP-included signboard costing and separately estimated signboards to determine the appropriate costing standards and prevent overpricing.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, in coordination with the Municipal Engineer, direct the:
Project Engineers to:
Strictly require all contractors to ensure that their entire workforce is wearing the required safety gears or PPE at all times within the construction project site for the protection and welfare of both the construction workers and the general public; and
Closely monitor the proper implementation of the CSHP and Standards by the contractors at the project sites by maintaining detailed records of contractor’s non-compliance per site visitation, which can be referenced during future payment processes.
Further, we recommended the Municipal Engineer to:
Formulate an internal policy that clearly defines the applicable sanctions or penalties for contractors found to be in violation of the CSHP, in order to ensure accountability and reinforce adherence to established safety protocols.
Standardize the computation of the costing of CSHP to ensure consistency, transparency and accountability and to prevent manipulation of project costs.

We reiterated the prior audit recommendations that the Municipal Mayor, being the Chairperson of the WATSAN Council, to:
Reactivate and mobilize the WATSAN Council to develop comprehensive guidelines and work plans addressing the issues of the six non-functional potable water supply systems;
Instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to formulate and implement sustainability measures aimed at ensuring the continued operation and potential expansion of the water supply systems. This includes providing targeted assistance such as financial support and capacity-building programs to the BAWASA and barangays that have yet to establish water supply association; and
Direct the Municipal Engineer to conduct regular visitation schedules to these potable water supply systems, enabling ongoing monitoring and the provision of technical assistance to barangay officials or BAWASA representatives.

We recommended that the Municipality, through the Municipal Treasurer, seek authority from the Sangguniang Bayan to establish a dedicated cash advance exclusively for the AICS program, in accordance with COA Circular No. 97-002; and
MSWDO to implement a streamlined processing workflow that would shorten the processing time of requirements and reflect the revision in the Citizen’s Charter.
Further, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the MSWDO, initiate a review of the existing ordinance and, if deemed necessary, propose amendments to Ordinance No. 337 to the Sangguniang Bayan to include clear and specific eligibility criteria for each type of assistance, define the conditions for granting minimum and maximum amounts and provide standardized schedules or ceilings per type of assistance to guide the social workers in their assessment, ensuring uniform implementation across all cases.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Dutifully and promptly compute the shares of the 37 barangays; and
Ensure the timely remittance of said shares within five (5) days after the end of each quarter, in strict compliance with Section 271(d) of the LGC, as amended; and
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the HRMO, consider the hiring of additional personnel to support the operations of the Accounting Office, subject to budget availability, to ensure full compliance with regulatory and audit requirements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the LSB to strictly monitor the submission and posting of SEF reports.  Specifically, the Municipal Mayor should instruct the LSB to:
Provide copies of the approved annual SEF Budget to the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer, copy furnished the Audit Team, within seven days from its approval, as their basis for disbursement or certification of availability of funds and for recording purposes; and
Submit quarterly and annual SEF Budget Accountability Form No. 1 to the DepEd CO, Sangguniang Bayan of Naguilian, La Union, and the Regional Offices of DBM and DILG not later than the 20th day after the end of the quarter and not later than the 15th day of February of the following year.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Treasurer to comply with the preparation and submission of the Schedule of Unreleased Checks to the Accounting Office at the end of each month or quarter for them to make the necessary adjustments to restore the amount of cash equivalent to the total amount of unreleased checks by debiting the accounts, Cash in Bank-LCCA, and crediting the appropriate payable/liability account. To ensure the fairness and correctness of the accounts appearing in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that Management instruct the Inventory Committee to comply with Item 5.11 of COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020. Submit the prepared Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) to the Audit Team at least ten (10) calendar days before the scheduled start of inventory taking activities for CY 2025 onwards.
We recommended that Management require the GSO to update PARs to monitor and control accountability over the issuance of PPE to end-users in accordance with Section 158 of COA Circular 92-386.

We recommended that Management submit the required Disbursement Vouchers and Approved Obligation Request with complete supporting documents for loan payments during the Calendar Year 2024. Likewise, on succeeding loan payments, ensure compliance with Item 5.3 of COA Circular No. 2021-014 dated December 22, 2021, to avoid suspension in an audit.

We recommended that Management properly fill out the PRs on the succeeding procurements of the Municipality to determine that the goods/services to be procured are within/or more than the approved budget of the Municipality, in accordance with the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual, Volume 2.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer to monitor and enforce the proper implementation of the CSHP by the contractors at the project sites. Also, require the contractors to ensure that their workers are wearing the required safety gear or protective equipment at all times within the construction project site for the protection and welfare of both the construction workers and the general public.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Engineer require the concerned contractors to comply and submit a tax clearance coming from the Provincial Treasurer that the corresponding sand and gravel fees have been paid before the final payment of infrastructure projects was made in compliance with Item 9.1.1.4 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.

We recommended that the Management consider utilizing the Quick Response Fund when there is a declaration of a state of calamity in compliance with the Disaster Response projects and activities specified under items 5.3.1 to 5.3.4 of NDRRMC, DBM, and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No.2013-1 dated March 25, 2013. Likewise, the fund should be utilized to help the affected constituents recover through the distribution of food subsidies, relief goods, and livelihood relief or assistance to victims.

We recommended that the Management coordinate with the Local School Board (LSB) to include the “Previous Year's Unexpended Balances” when preparing for the SEF Budget Preparation Form No. 1 (Annex A) to implement more programs, projects, and activities for the intended beneficiaries.

We recommended that Management after the election ban prioritize and consider filling-up the vacant positions by hiring new employees or expediting the promotion of deserving employees to enhance its services to attain full implementation of programs, plans, and activities of the local government unit for the optimal benefit of the constituents as well as to provide equal job opportunities to the community. Further, personnel movement has to be effected in order to acknowledge the exemplary performance of employees by way of promotion to higher rank thereby allowing the entry of new applicants for lower level positions.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Reduce the RPT/SET receivables and their corresponding deferred accounts whenever income from advance payments is recognized in its appropriate period;
Prepare the following adjusting journal entries:
GF Books
SEF Books
We also recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Accountant to review past accounting journal entries related to advance payments, and identify if there are understatements in the RPT/SET receivables and their corresponding deferred accounts. Additionally, effect adjusting entries, if needed.

We recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Compute and consistently provide the correct depreciation for the road network system of which no residual value shall be recognized;
Prepare the following adjusting journal entry:
Restate the CY 2023 Consolidated Financial Statements by reflecting the correct depreciation expenses for CYs 2018 to 2023 in all the affected components of the same; and
Comply with the disclosure requirements on the Road Network System to ensure that relevant information is available to the intended users of the financial statements.
We likewise recommended that the LCE create an Inventory Committee who shall prepare a Physical Inventory Plan containing the specific assignments/duties of the committee members, the cut-off date and a schedule specifying the dates and locations of the inventory taking activities from start to finish.
Thereafter, we recommended that the LCE enjoin the Inventory Committee, General Services Officer (GSO) and Municipal Accountant to undertake the procedural guidelines for the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances to come up with more reliable account balances to be presented in the financial statements.
Lastly, we reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation for the LCE to direct the GSO and Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain Property Cards and PPELC, respectively, for each class of PPE.

We recommended that the LCE direct the:
Municipal Accountant to adjust the Cash in Bank, LCCA and other affected accounts pertaining to the misstatements;
Municipal Treasurer to cancel the unclaimed stale checks and furnish the Municipal Accountant with the duplicate copies of DVs and its supporting documents for the preparation of the Journal Entry Voucher to ensure recording of cancellation, and call the attention or inform the payees of those cancelled checks to surrender the stale checks and issue new checks thereof; and
Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to regularly reconcile their records to ensure the correct cash balance presented in the financial statements.

We recommended that the LCE direct the end-user to fast-track the completion of the required supporting documents by communicating with the concerned departments, taking into account the actions to address the cause of the delay in the process, and strictly monitor all unpaid obligations to ensure that deficiencies are addressed promptly, and paid in a timely manner in accordance with the terms agreed upon in the PO or contracts,  and the standard operating procedures provided in the system procedures and/or Citizen’s Charter.

We recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, in coordination with the concerned Department Heads, to evaluate the status of the balances of each PPA under the continuing appropriations and initiate re-programming of such according to the prevailing budget needs of the municipality.
Further, we recommended that the LCE direct the:
Municipal Accountant to prepare the trial balance and financial statements for the 20% DF using the available utilization report and SLs; and
Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer to properly reflect the re-programmed continuing appropriation as part of the current appropriation in the Statement of Comparison of Budget and Actual Amount.

We recommended that the LCE direct the MPDC and the Municipal Agriculturist to prioritize and ensure that the PPAs programmed from the shares of collections of tobacco excise taxes are exclusively intended for the benefit of NTA-registered tobacco farmers.
We also recommended that the MPDC and the Municipal Agriculturist, in coordination with the barangay councils, to conduct public consultations or assemblies with the tobacco farmers to facilitate and provide tailored support and assistance that will address their needs effectively.

We recommended that the LCE, being the Chairman of the LSB, to observe strict compliance with the Joint Circulars on the utilization of SEF.
Further, we recommended that the LSB prioritize PPAs as provided in the abovementioned Joint Circulars to ensure that resources are efficiently channeled towards initiatives that support the educational needs of their constituents.

We recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the GSO, to insure all the insurable assets of the municipality to protect such from any fortuitous events, and prepare and submit the consolidated PIF listing of all the insurable properties and other asset to the Audit Team Leader. We further recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Budget Officer to allocate funds intended for the insurance of other insurable assets.

We recommended that the LCE, in coordination with the Municipal Engineer, direct the Project Engineers to:
Strictly require all contractors to ensure their entire workforce are wearing the required safety gear or personal protection equipment within the construction project site at all times for the protection and welfare of both the construction workers and the general public;
Closely monitor the proper implementation of the CSHP and standards by the contractors at the project sites by maintaining detailed records of non-compliance of the contractor per site visitation, which can be use as reference during future payment processes; and
Formulate an internal policy that clearly defines the appropriate sanctions or penalties to be applied to contractors found in violation of the required CHSP to ensure accountability and adherence to safety protocols.

We recommended that the LCE direct the:
Municipal Engineer to require the contractors to conduct material testing for infrastructure projects to guarantee that no substandard construction materials are utilized, thereby ensuring that the quality of projects aligns with the stringent standards set by the DPWH; and
Municipal Accountant to implement a policy requiring the submission of material testing results prior to effect any payment to contractors.

We recommended that the LCE direct the BAC to ensure compliance in the posting of NOA and NTP in the PhilGEPS within the prescribed time.
We also recommended that the LCE instruct the BAC to comply with their jury duty in adherence to the principles of transparency, accountability and good governance in the government procurement process.

We recommended that the LCE direct the:
Municipal Engineer to enforce strict compliance that all awarded PPAs should be provided with signboards, and that the signboards shall contain all the relevant information and details; and that the project status is maintained as current and updated; and
Inform both the SA and ATL within ten days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the program/activity, that the appropriate project signboards and/or public notices are already posted, thereby facilitating the validation of the same.

We recommended that the LCE/HOPE direct the:
a. 	BAC and Municipal Accountant to refrain from paying BAC Honoraria out of the excess collections from the proceeds of the sale of bid documents of the previous year to ensure that payment made is on a legal basis; and
b. 	Municipal Accountant to record, in the ensuing years, liquidated damages using the income account as Miscellaneous Income.

We recommended that the LCE, in coordination with the HRMO and Department Heads, to:
Revisit and study the organizational structure of the municipality particularly the staffing pattern of all its offices, and consider abolishing items that are not considered vital, specifically those that have been left vacant for a long period; and
Fill all vacant positions that are still considered vital by hiring new employees or expediting the promotion of deserving employees.
We also recommended that the LCE, in coordination with the HRMO, to:
Issue an office order to renew the designations of the Head of the GSO and Head of the MDRRMO, copy furnish the Audit Team; and
Effectively monitor the office designations of the officials and employees of the municipality by ensuring timely renewal before its expiration.

We recommended that Management properly plan their future procurement by being updated and well-versed with relevant issuances affecting their projects.  In cases where the program is spearheaded by another government agency, Management shall coordinate closely with said partner agency to ensure compliance with rules and regulation, and take advantage of the assistance being offered to maintain economy and efficiency in the use of public resources.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to make necessary adjusting entries to record the reversion of stale checks in the General Fund totaling P8,912.62 pursuant to Section 97 of PD 1445.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant that regular purchases of inventory of drugs and medicines during the year should be recorded and coursed thru the appropriate inventory accounts as provided in Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-009, thereby providing inventory control accounts in the books of the agency.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the appropriate adjusting journal entries to correct the misstatements of the affected accounts. Henceforth, ensure that the construction of infrastructure asset should be capitalized or recognized as property, plant and equipment in accordance with IPSAS No. 17. Likewise, for future Approved Annual Budget, the LGU should use the funds exclusively for the specific purpose for which they have been appropriated.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to make necessary adjusting entries for the affected accounts to correct the aforementioned misstatements in the financial report. Likewise, for succeeding transactions of the LGU, strictly account transactions with the appropriate account title as recommended in the revised chart of accounts for LGUs as provided in COA Circular No. 2015-009.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant review the proper classification of the concerned accounts totaling P575,000.00 in compliance with Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-009  and draw the necessary journal entries to adjust the same from Due to LGU to Trust Liabilities account.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to comply with the rules set forth in COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.  The LGU should stop using the cash advances for operating expenses account and should properly establish petty cash or regular cash advance in accordance with the rules on granting and utilization of regular cash advances under the same Circular.
Lastly, we recommended Management for settlement of noted deficiencies of P135,432.68 if found to be valid or appropriate upon reconciliation of Accounting records against PhilHealth records.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and other concerned Municipal Officials to allocate 1% of the NTA for the strengthening and implementation of the PPAs of the LCPC and strictly adhere to the provisions of Section 15 of the Republic Act 9344 and DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120.

We recommended that the Management require the contractors of the projects with defects to immediately undertake the necessary correction/rectification of the deficiencies indicated in the COA-STAS report as summarized. Likewise, for the succeeding infrastructure contracts of the Municipality, the date of the contracts should be at the date of receipt of the “Notice to Proceed” and “Statement of Work Accomplished” should be prepared by the contractor as provided in Section 37.4.1 and Item 5, Annex E of Revised RA 9184, respectively.

We recommended that the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council (MDRRMC) continuously monitors and evaluates the utilization of the LDRRMF especially the Mitigation Fund to ensure the timely implementation of all the pre-disaster preparedness programs, projects, and activities.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended Management: 

Adhere strictly to the provisions of DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-01 on the appropriation, utilization and programming of the 20% of the IRA for development projects. Consider utilizing the fund for projects that yield lasting benefits to the constituents of the Municipality. Likewise, expenditures that are not investment or capital in nature like road widening/re-graveling be charged under the General Fund Proper.

The Municipal Engineer to prepare the ABC and detailed estimates of the infrastructure projects before the start of budget call of the Municipality in accordance with Section 7 of the 2016  revised Implementing Rules and Regulation of R.A. No. 9184  to avoid idle funds in the 20% DF.

The Municipal Budget Officer to propose and coordinate with other offices concerned for the re-appropriation of the accumulated savings from completed PPAs to development programs and projects that need immediate attention; and

The Municipal Planning and Development Officer and Municipal Engineer shall initiate the needed coordination, monitoring, and evaluation of the implementation of the PPAs under the 20% DF to assure that all unimplemented PPAs be given immediate action and implementation or reprogram the funds of these PPAs to more important developmental projects as required by current circumstances and trends.

Direct the Municipal General Services Officer to prioritize procurement of regular or ordinary office supplies first from PS-DBM. observations.recommendation:
In this regard, we recommended that Management:

Submit justification why the 10% profit was still included in the estimated unit cost presented in the BOM for the project despite being implemented by administration for further evaluation; and

Stop the practice of adding 10% profit and 12% VAT (if the estimated unit cost presented is already inclusive of VAT) to the total estimated cost of materials.  Other necessary expenses if any, should be presented separately and in detail.
observations.managementAction:
The Management justified that their basis in adding up a percentage profit, is the Department of Public Works and Highways (DPWH) Department Order No. 197 Series of 2016 Revised Guidelines in the Preparation of Approved Budget for the Contract (ABC) particularly item B.4- which reads: Contractor’s Profit Margin-shall be 8% of the Estimated Direct Cost (EDC) for projects above P5 million and 10% for projects and P5 million and below because they thought that the same presentation is adapted even the projects were implemented by administration.

Also, the Management  already stopped the practice of adding 10% profit and 12% VAT and the other necessary expenses if any, were presented separately and in details.

Instruct the concerned officials to furnish the Audit Team with copies of contracts and its supporting documents especially infrastructure projects, within five (5) days after its perfection pursuant to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2009-001; and

We recommended that BAC require the winning bidders to submit the appropriate format of certification by the Insurance Commission authorizing the surety or insurance company to issue such performance security, which is specific to every awarded project as required by Section 39.2 of the revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that BAC strictly comply with the procedures on receipts, opening and preliminary examination of bids as provided for in the Procurement Manual for LGUs and the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that Management:
The BAC upon receipt of the Purchase Request shall check and verify whether the items requested specify or with reference to brand names, if so, return the PR to the Office concerned for correction and modification; and
The BAC and the concerned Officials shall strictly comply with the provisions of RA 9184 and its RIRR in all its procurement activities to prevent incurrence of irregular expenditures.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that Management:
Initiate the preparation of necessary documents (eg. detailed engineering) to support cost estimate of projects funded under the 20% Development Plan before the budget preparation:
Require the Municipal Development Council to specify the details of the identified priority projects to be charged against the 20% Development Fund to avoid generic and lump-sum projects: and
Stop the practice of implementing generic or lump-sum projects without further authority from the Sanggunian to avoid future audit suspension/disallowance.
We recommended that Management strictly utilize the LDRRMF for implementation of DRRM Programs and regular Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses are to be sourced from other funds.

We recommended that Management:
Refrain from payment of procurement of goods with significant amounts through reimbursement basis, instead adhere to Section 93 of PD No. 1445 on direct payment to creditors/supplier of goods;
Strictly comply with the procedures under RA 9184; and
In the future, attach distribution list or list of affected families to disbursement vouchers as required by Section 4(6) of PD 1445.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the Municipal General Services Officer to prioritize procurement of regular or ordinary office supplies first from PS-DBM. Shopping from private suppliers should be resorted to only if the items being purchased are not available in the Procurement Service Depot. The Agency Procurement Request (APR) duly acted by the PS-DBM and the Price Quotation and stocks Availability Certification must be attached in the disbursement vouchers;
Direct the concerned officials and employees to be meticulous and judicious in the preparation of their Annual Procurement Plan so that procurements made by the Municipality are properly planned and executed, and procurements made through alternative methods do not result in splitting of government contracts; and
Stop the practice of splitting purchase requisitions or contracts on the purchase of office supplies pursuant to Section 54.1 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184, to promote effective competition and transparency in the procurement process and obtain a fair and equitable contract for the best interest of the Municipality.

We recommended that Management:
Submit written justification/explanation why the above-mentioned contract include provision of additional 6% mark-up price for the purchases on credit;
In the future, contract for the purchase of POL should be at retail pump price only as required by Annex A Part V (c)(i)(b) of GPPB Resolution No. 24-2019; and
Strengthen the control measures to ensure that all resources of the Municipality shall be safeguard against loss or wastage through improper disposition.
We recommended that Management secure copies of the MOA/TA from the source agencies and furnish the COA Audit Team copies of it for reference and audit purposes.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that Management:
Submit written justification/explanation on the payments of daily travel expenses. Otherwise, the payment made totaling P82,800.00 found to be excessive should be refunded; and
Avoid granting daily travel expenses more than what is allowed as stipulated in Section 5(b), (c), (e) of Executive Order No. 77.

In view of the foregoing,   we recommended that Management:
Immediately appoint or designate a new OSCA Head pursuant to Section 2, Article 21, Rule VI of IRR of R.A. No. 9994;
Stop the payment of salary to OSCA Head awaiting the newly designated or appointed OSCA Head; and
Strictly comply with the provisions of IRR of R.A. No. 9994, otherwise known as “Expanded Senior Citizens Act of 2010”.

observations.recommendation:
Given the foregoing observations, we have recommended and Management assured the audit team to:
Strictly adhere to the accounting and reporting guidelines on property management as provided in the MNGAS,

Also, for the Municipal General Services Officer to prepare stock cards for all the inventory items to aid in the periodic reconciliation of accounting and perpetual records.

We have recommended and Management agreed that the Acting Municipal Accountant employ added efforts to locate records that would support these liabilities.  If no records could be found, request the Sangguniang Bayan to revert the same to the unappropriated surplus of the Municipality through a Resolution.

We recommended Management to comply with IPSAS 16 in the recording of Investment Property. The Acting Municipal Accountant shall record in the books the parcel of lots referred to above and ground floor of the Balikbayan Building as Investment Property instead of Land and Building to reflect the correct balances of the affected accounts at year-end.

We recommended that the misclassified accounts be adjusted by the Acting Municipal Accountant in conformity with the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs for fair presentation of the financial statements.

We recommend that Management, through the Municipal Development Council ensure that development funds are utilized through proper prioritization and planning the PPAs to be implemented to achieve the desirable socio-economic development and environmental management for the benefit of the constituents. The local finance committee shall likewise consider availing credit facilities without needing to allocate an enormous portion of their Internal Revenue Allotment to repay loans leaving a lesser portion for the delivery of essential public service.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Local School Board to prepare a comprehensive annual supplemental budget for the operation and maintenance of public schools within the municipality in accordance with Section 272 of RA No. 7160 giving priority to the construction and repair of school buildings, facilities and equipment, educational research, purchase of books and periodicals, and sports development. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Local GAD Focal Person through the Municipal Planning and Development Office to comply with the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular 2013-01 and 2016-01 on the establishment of a gender responsive monitoring and evaluation system through the creation of GAD M&E Team to evaluate the outcomes of the LGU GAD policies, programs, and projects and submit a GAD Evaluation Report to the DILG Regional Office at the end of the LCE’s three-year term.

We recommended that the SWM Board of the municipal government adopt measures to ensure the viability and effective implementation of solid waste management programs to address the environmental concerns on solid waste management and to enhance its efforts towards public health and environmental protection.

Considering the substantial investment involved in the provision of potable water system projects and in cognizance of the equally significant impact of the projects in addressing the plight of waterless communities, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Engineering Office to enhance monitoring of the facilities to determine the necessary course of actions which can be undertaken to address the current issues and concerns involving the water systems. Also, provide annual operations and maintenance funds through a Sangguniang Bayan Resolution to cover the repair and rehabilitation of these water facilities.

We have recommended to the Head of the Procuring Entity to direct the BAC to:
Submit justification in declaring the contractors as Lowest Calculated Responsive Bidder despite the foregoing deficiencies noted; and
Judiciously conduct post-qualification by verifying, validating, and ascertaining all statements made and documents submitted by the bidder with the Lowest Calculated Bid and ensure that the Post-Qualification Report contains in-depth results on the verification of technical requirements.

We recommended the Management to refrain from granting loan assistance to certain individuals and to grant only assistance to those who are really entitled to it in view of the well-settled principle on the general welfare clause in the LGC and to the fundamental principle on financial transactions that government funds should be spent or used solely for public purposes.
Also, the management shall immediately collect the full amount of the loan receivables amounting to ₱2,965,753.12.

We recommended the Management to adhere to the provisions of the MNGAS for LGUs with regard to collection, remittance and recording of revenues. The management should implement measures to ensure prompt reporting and avoid delays in remittances of collections in order to reflect the correct amount of collections at a given date, practice timely deposit of collections and avoid the risk of possible loss and misuse.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to automatically release the barangay shares of RPT on a quarterly basis in compliance to Section 271 (d) of RA 7160 and the prompt release of financial aid to augment their meager funds to finance their operations.

We recommended that Management thru the coordinated efforts of the Municipal Assessor and Municipal Treasurer should fast track the completion of the list of taxpayers pertaining to delinquent RPT as basis of the Municipal Accountant to record it in the books.

We recommended that Management ensures that all moneys due to the LGU and officially receive by the collecting officers are acknowledge with official receipt in full amount and accounted for as government funds.  Further, all disbursement of government funds shall be supported with complete documentation.
We recommended that Management create and update their Ordinances fixing the rates and conditions on the use of the LGU’s agricultural equipment.
We recommended that Management through the Municipal Budget Officer should ensure that the allotment for Capital Outlay should be expended according to the description of its allotment class.

We recommended that the LGU conduct public bidding or any alternative method as may be applicable in the circumstances consistent with RA 9184 and its Revised IRR, for the procurement of its fuel requirements for service vehicle and development projects.

We recommended that Management should implement the Construction of Sanitary Landfill if still warranted by the current condition.
Review the circumstances that made the LGU to resort to recurring re-programming of projects.  Adopt measures to ascertain adequate planning of PPAs.
We recommended that Management revisit the guidelines on the utilization of 20% development fund and ensure that it is utilized consistent with DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor create an Inventory Committee to conduct a physical inventory of all properties and prepare the RCPPE using the prescribed format. The physical count of PPE should be undertaken annually by the Inventory Committee and the corresponding RCPPE be submitted to the Audit Team not later than January 31 of each year as required under Section 124 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I. 

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal General Services Officer prepare, update and maintain the required property cards and ledger cards and cause the reconciliation of results of the actual physical count with the property and accounting records. All discrepancies between the physical inventory and the accounting records must be investigated, adjusted and reconciled immediately. 

Moreover, we recommended that the Inventory Committee ensure to check the condition of the PPEs.  Any damaged, obsolete and unserviceable property shall be reported in the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) for the immediate and proper disposal of the unserviceable PPEs.

The Head of the Office for Senior Citizens Affairs (OSCA Head) was not chosen from the three (3) nominees recommended by the general assembly of senior citizens in the Municipality and was appointed by the Municipal Mayor through a Contract of Service covering the period of May 2, 2018 to December 31, 2018 with a fixed monthly honorarium of P6,600.00 contrary to Article 21, Rule VI of the Implementing Rules and Regulations of R.A. No. 9994 or the “Expanded Senior Citizens Act of 2010”, hence the regularity, validity and propriety of the appointment and payment of honorarium is affected.

We have recommended that the Management:

Strictly adhere to the proper steps of appointment as mandated by Republic Act No. 9994.

Personnel under Job Order Contract (JOC)/Contract of Service (COS) numbering 98 were almost the number of filled plantilla positions which casts doubt on the necessity of hiring Job Orders/Contracts of Services.

We have recommended that the following to the Local Chief Executive:

Revisit the organization structure of the municipality so as to create positions which are essential in the delivery of public service to the constituents and to lessen the number of JOCs;

Submit/attach Accomplishment Reports and certification that the activities/services cannot be provided by regular and permanent personnel to prove the validity and legality of the disbursements pertaining to their salaries; and

Strictly comply with the abovementioned provisions on the hiring of JOCs.

observations.recommendation:
The Municipality failed to setup its Local Economic Enterprise (LEEs) in accordance with the Department of Budget and Management (DBM) Local Budget Circular (LBC) No. 11 dated June 10, 2016, thus, the financial self-reliance and viability of those LEEs could not be evaluated

We have recommended that Management, in accordance with LBC No. 111, consider the full adoption of the guide in operating its existing LEEs in view of their critical roles in the LGU development and their significant impact to good local governance.
observations.reason: The LGU is on the process of evaluating possible LEE to be recognized

observations.recommendation:
The required posting of warranty security for all completed infrastructure projects was not strictly complied with, thus, there was no assurance against structural defects and failures within the period ranging from two to fifteen years from final acceptance. Further, Certificates of Final Acceptance were not issued to contractors after one year from project completion.

We have recommended and the Municipal Engineer agreed that he:

Will always require posting of warranty security in any acceptable form for completed infrastructure projects in accordance with Section 62 of the 2016 IRR of RA 9184; 

Henceforth establish a system and maintain records on specific warranty periods and security for each procurement of civil works.

We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Inspectorate Team to conduct inspection of completed projects after the one (1) year defects liability period and issue the corresponding Certificate of Final Acceptance to the contractors if no defect /deficiency was detected.
observations.managementAction: Management said that they will reorganize the inspection and monitoring team of the LGU and conduct the inspection of completed projects
observations.reason: Review of relevant documents shows that the recommendation is implemented

observations.recommendation:
The Approved Budget for the Contract (ABC) for infrastructure projects implemented by the municipality for CY 2018 was not prepared in accordance with the prescribed format as provided under DPWH Department Order (DO) No. 197 series of 2016 dated October 7, 2016 and Annex A of the 2016 Revised IRR of Republic Act (RA) No. 9184 rendering difficulty in the cost analysis and in the price evaluation of the project. 

We have recommended that the Municipal Engineer prepare the Approved Budget for the Contract of infrastructure projects in accordance with the latest guidelines prescribed by the DPWH, which to date is the DPWH Department Order No. 197, series of 2016 dated October 7, 2016 and Annex A of the 2016 Revised IRR of Republic Act No. 9184 to facilitate proper cost analysis.

Audit Team not later than January 31 of each year as required under Section 124
of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal
General Services Officer prepare, update and maintain the required property
cards and ledger cards and cause the reconciliation of results of the actual
physical count with the property and accounting records. All discrepancies
between the physical inventory and the accounting records must be investigated,
adjusted and reconciled immediately.

Moreover, we recommended that the Inventory Committee ensure to
check the condition of the PPEs. Any damaged, obsolete and unserviceable
property shall be reported in the Inventory and Inspection Report of
Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) for the immediate and proper disposal of the
unserviceable PPEs.

Accountant to prepare a journal entry to recognize the completed projects under

Nos. 1 and 17 and DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2018-47 for fair

We have recommended that in order to enforce collection, Management
send demand letters to the last known addresses of the borrowers and thereafter
exhaust all remedies to collect the receivables. We recommended further that for
fair presentation of receivables, Management assess the actual status of dormant
receivables; confirm with existing entities and request for write-off in
accordance with Commission on Audit Circular No. 2016-005 dated December
19, 2016, if applicable.

Management was also enjoined to adhere with the general and specific
guidelines cited in COA Circular 2016-005 in order to minimize the incidence of
significant receivables falling past due.

We have recommended that the management strictly comply with the
guidelines set in GPPB Circular No. 05-2016 dated December 20, 2016 with
regard to the period for the conduct of negotiated procurement under Two
Failed Biddings modality for timely implementation of projects. Likewise,
Management adhere to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June
14, 2012 to facilitate complete documentation for transactions paid by the
municipality.

We have recommended that Management adhere strictly with the above-
cited provisions of the Manual on NGAS, Volume I, as well as the provisions of
COA Circular No. 2015-009 on the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs.
Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the
necessary adjusting entries to reclassify the insurance expense account and
recognize the Prepaid Insurance account for the unexpired portion thereof.

We have recommended that Management:
a. Stop the practice of using the RATA as payment for the monthly loan
amortizations of their employees; and
b. Strictly comply with the pertinent provisions of DBM Local Budget
Circular No. 103 dated May 15, 2013.

We have recommended that Management:
a. Discontinue the practice of including loan repayments/amortization to
private lending institutions as deduction from the payroll; and
b. Monitor the payroll deductions and refrain from signing certifications
for loan applications should an employee’s net take home pay falls
below the mandatory amount of P5,000.00.

We have recommended that the Acting Municipal Accountant strictly
comply with the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units in
recording and reporting financial transactions of the municipality as prescribed
under COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.

We have recommended that the Municipal School Board limit the
utilization of the SEF to the priority programs and projects defined under R.A.
No. 7160 and DepEd, DBM and DILG Joint Circular No. 01, s. 2017, in order to
maximize the benefits that local schools could derived from this fund.

We have recommended that Management ensure utmost compliance and
adopt measures to facilitate the submission of their accounts on time as
prescribed in the foregoing Section of COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated
September 15, 2009 to enable timely evaluation of the transactions’ propriety,
completeness and accuracy. Further, we recommend that the Municipality
consider reviewing the plantilla of the Accounting Office in order to address the
need of additional personnel.

We have recommended that the concerned municipal officials and
employees fully implement programs/projects/activities funded under the GAD
Plan and Budget to fully address gender issues/problems of the constituents.
Further, for the GFPS to ensure and monitor the effective implementation of the
annual GAD PPAs. Lastly, to coordinate with other agencies in order to avoid
overlapping of programs/projects/activities pertaining to GAD.

observations.recommendation:
We, therefore, recommended that in order to enforce collection,
Management send demand letters to the last known addresses of the borrowers and
thereafter exhaust all remedies to collect the receivables. We recommended further
that for fair presentation of receivables, Management assess the actual status of
dormant receivables; confirm with existing entities and request for write-off in
accordance with Commission on Audit Circular No. 2016-005 dated December
19, 2016, if applicable.

Management is also enjoined to adhere with the general and specific
guidelines cited in COA Circular 2016-005 in order to minimize the incidence of
significant receivables falling past due.

We, therefore, recommended and management agreed that the Accounting
Office review all the information pertaining to the Work/Zoo Animals and make the
necessary adjustments in the accounts. We further recommend that henceforth,
Management ensure compliance with the measurement and disclosure requirements
under PPSAS 27 after the initial recognition of biological assets in the books.

We, therefore recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the
Accounting Office to immediately prepare and submit the bank reconciliation
statements along with attachments pertaining to the period July to December 2017.
We recommend further that henceforth, the person responsible ensure the
preparation and submission of BRS within the prescribed period in accordance with
Section 3 of COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996 for faithful
representation of the cash accounts of the municipality as well as to avoid any
sanction in the future.

We recommended that management henceforth release the RPT shares of the
barangays within five days after the end of each quarter and shall not be subject to
any lien or holdback for whatever purpose in conformity with Section 271 (d) of RA
7160 in order for the barangays to utilize these funds for the benefits of their
constituents and to avoid any sanction in the future.

We reiterated our recommendation that management submit all perfected
contracts and POs along with all the required attachments within five (5) days from
perfection as provided under COA Circular No. 2009-001. This will enable the Audit
Team to conduct timely evaluation of contracts and review of items purchased and
thereafter immediately communicate to management any defect/deficiency noted
thereon.

We, therefore recommended that the Local Chief Executive select from
qualified senior citizens and thereafter appoint a new OSCA Head, not only to fully
comply with the IRR of the Senior Citizens Act of 2010 but also to accord equal
opportunity to others who may be able to similarly contribute to the total well-being
of the elderly and their participation in the society.

We recommended and the Municipal Engineer agreed that he:
o Will always require posting of warranty security in any acceptable form
for completed infrastructure projects in accordance with Section 62 of
the 2016 IRR of RA 9184;
o Henceforth establish a system and maintain records on specific warranty
periods and security for each procurement of civil works.

We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the
Inspectorate Team to conduct inspection of completed projects after the one (1) year
defects liability period and issue the corresponding Certificate of Final Acceptance
to the contractors if no defect /deficiency was detected.

We therefore recommended and management agreed to submit justification
on the above-noted observation and ensure strict compliance to the provisions
stipulated in Annex E of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184. Henceforth, ensure that
all requests for time extensions are supported by written consent of bondsmen to
correspondingly extend the validity of the performance security for the protection of
the Procuring Entity.

We therefore recommended and management agreed that henceforth, the
Local Chief Executive through the BAC, fully comply with the provisions of the
2016 IRR of RA 9184 specifically for all the municipality’s procurements done
through alternative modes of procurement to avoid any sanction in the future.

We, therefore, recommended and Management agreed to ensure the
availability of resources and inclusion of the lacking items in the formulation of the
LDRRMFIP in compliance with the minimum preparedness standards set forth
under the NDPP not only to support the initiatives of the DILG but most
significantly to avert the loss of lives during disasters.

We, therefore, recommended that Management, in accordance with LBC No.
111, consider the full adoption of the guide in operating its existing LEEs in view of
their critical roles in the LGU development and their significant impact to good
local governance.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal Treasurer’s
Office:
• Continue to demand settlement of tax liabilities from the identified
taxpayers;
• Document the main reasons why these remained uncollected for the
past years;
• Adopt corresponding strategies to address the specific concerns of the
taxpayers; and
• In coordination with the Provincial Treasury Office, avail of the
administrative and legal remedies provided by law to enforce
collection of the delinquent real property taxes.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that in order to effortlessly impose responsibility of the contractor for any structural defect, the Municipal Engineer:
•	Issue Certificate of Final Acceptance one year after completion of the infrastructure projects;
•	Require posting of warranty security in any acceptable form from one year after final acceptance of infrastructures implemented; and
•	Henceforth establish a system and maintain records on specific warranty periods and security for each procurement of civil works.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Engineering Office is currently conducting inventory of infrastructure projects and currently communicating with the different contractors to establish a system and maintain records on specific warranty periods and security for each procurement of civil works.
observations.reason: Imposition of warranty securities from final acceptance did not cover all the projects supposedly covered against structural defects.

observations.recommendation: We, therefore, recommended and Management agreed to formulate and adopt a CPES-based system of monitoring and contractors evaluation to ensure that infrastructure projects conform with the specified requirements of projects.
